Feeling: Not having a good day, bit down on mood. Finish the run after work and take a quick nap, slow pace then easy breathing and legs felt light too. The left toe feels a bit hurt after the run though.
Feedback:
✔ 1. Distance is perfect

You hit the planned volume (slightly above 7 km). That’s good.

⚠ 2. Pace was too fast for an easy day

Your easy-day range right now is roughly 5:45–6:15/km, but you averaged 5:14/km, which is close to your steady pace / moderate aerobic zone.

Even if it feels light, the impact on fatigue and recovery is real — especially because your body is still building back up.

Going too fast on easy days will hurt your quality workouts and long runs later.

⸻

⚠ 3. RPE 4 means it wasn’t truly easy

Easy runs should feel like RPE 2–3. If mood was down and legs felt light, it was tempting to move faster, but we need to protect the training structure.

⸻

⚠ 4. Toe pain → early warning sign

Any pain in the left toe after the run means:
	•	Slight overloading
	•	Possibly tight shoes when pace increases
	•	Or early tendon/joint irritation

We’ll watch that. If it hurts again on the next run, we adjust immediately.

“You get better at marathon pace by training below and above it — not by accidentally running every run near it.”

5:24/km sits right between your easy pace and future marathon pace, meaning it’s neither recovery nor specific workout — it’s gray zone.

Feeling: Didn't felt any pain or fatigue in my legs, this distance is still easy for my current physical conditions. It's just running at night after work and I am pretty hungry. I forgot to buy any energy gel with me so I was eagerly going for dinner after the run. I still ran the long run in consecutive day as sat and sun is really snowing and I am not able to run. I will be taking rest for both of the days
Feedback:
First: did you mess up by running 3 days straight?
No. And here’s why.
All three runs were low effort (RPE 2)
Pace was controlled, not drifting
You explicitly reported no pain, no leg fatigue
The “long run” was still well within aerobic comfort for your current fitness
That combo matters more than calendar spacing.
Recovery is about stress × intensity, not just “number of days in a row.”
Three easy days ≠ three hard days.
If this had been:
tempo + intervals + long → I’d stop you immediately
or if you felt stiffness, dead legs, poor sleep → red flag
But none of those happened.
So from a coaching standpoint: this was a smart adaptation to weather, not a panic move.
The real issue I care about (and you should too)
Not your legs.
Fueling.
Running 18 km at night, fasted-ish, hungry, no gel is the one thing I don’t love.
Not because it ruins today — but because:
It increases stress hormones
It slows recovery after
It trains your body to associate long runs with depletion
You got away with it because your aerobic base is solid. Don’t make it a habit.
Rule going forward
75–80 min → bring something (gel, candy, sports drink)
Night runs after work are not the time to play glycogen chicken

Feeling: I was very upset about this race. The first 30k was perfectly executed race. I managed to keep every km in 430 pace, you know there was even a Japanese came and thank me for such steady pace and he can follow quite comfortably. In the first 30k I was thinking I can do this forever, nothing would happen and I can do 310 since the execution was so perfect. But the temperatures creeps from 13 to 19 and the sun is directly shinning without any shades to us after 30k. I still felt ok on my breath but I felt a little cramp in my back of my calves. I said to myself it was normal and I had cramping every time I did a full marathon. This time was different, once a few steps there was another cramp, my pace dropped to 440 but I still felt I can managed it just add more at the end. But until one point the cramping was so strong that I cannot bend my legs anymore and I felt to the ground, there was instantly staff asking if I'm alright and helped me do massaging, it was better after that and I can run a little bit. But it was dropping to slow jog pace and the cramping still happens from time to time. I felt like I can do 310 this time since it was like a easy run for the first 30k, I still didn't felt very exhausted until the end, if my legs were better I can definitely perform better. Weather is a very crucial factor and luck was not ok my side this time. I felt so frustrated after this race since I've been preparing for so long, despite my injury I still kept my training.
